module: Module 1 |Introduction to Sustainability Matters in SMEs
content: I welcome you all to this guest speaker session with Dr. Sumitra Chowdhury. I would start with introducing Dr. Sumitra to our audience. Sumitra Chowdhury is an economic policy strategist and advisor, and a former officer of the Indian Economic Service and Director of Financial Stability, Department of Economic Affairs, Ministry of Finance, Government of India. Her career has been a journey of economic leadership and international influence shaping India's macroeconomic policies and financial sector regulations to present to representing the nation in global financial and economic forums. In a distinguished career of the Indian Economic Service, she spearheaded thoughts and policies in the Ministry of Finance, Ministry of External Affairs, Ministry of Consumer Affairs, food and Public Distribution, and Ministry of Agriculture and Farmer's Welfare. She played a key role in safeguarding financial sector resilience during COVID-19 by spearheading the agenda setting of the Financial Stability and Development Council in coordination with the financial sector regulators. She represented India in the meetings of Multilateral Institutions and forums, including the World Bank IMF, Financial Stability Board, EU, G20, BRICS, ASEAN, SAARC, BIMSTEC, G24, and ensured that India's priorities were built in the decisions taken. She was instrumental in formulating the memorandum of association of the new development bank and the contingent reserve arrangement of BRICS. She advanced India's economic interest in the bilateral financial and economic dialogues and promoted India's foreign investments and trade. She has authored numerous policy papers, research publications, and government reports that have shaped macroeconomic and regulatory policy strategies at both national and international levels. As a seasoned propent of data-driven policymaking, her work has influenced high level decisions shaping domestic regulatory landscapes and international financial disclosure. Committed to furthering inclusive and stable growth through sectoral and state level policies aligned with national goals and citizen aspiration targeting to emerge as a developed economy by 2047. Her areas of interest include Financial Inclusion, Climate Finance Regulation, FinTech and ai, economic diplomacy to promote investment and trade to support one district, one product. Innovative financing for health and education. She also has an interest in policy research and policy advocacy. We welcome you, Ma'am. Thank you for joining us today. Thank you. Thank you so much. Thank you. After a very informative session or the introduction of Dr. Sumitra I think it is, it should be a great session for our learners to think about what would be the takeaways that they want to envisage from this you know, entire segment that we have brought in front of you because Dr. Sumitra really has a wide ranging a spectrum of work that she has taken part in throughout her public service. She's now retired from public service, but she continues to engage with all of us for meaningful engagement with society. Thank you so much again for joining us. We will straightaway dive into the question. The first question being how would you define SMEs in India and when did they start to gain visibility? So thank you so much both of you, professor Deepti and Riya and all the students for joining. I never leave an opportunity to interact with the youngsters because I think that because of our association, even if two, three minds also get changed that is the contribution which we should be looking forward to. This is a subject small and medium enterprises. You know, this is a subject which initially I used to work when I just first joined the government of India. I was in the Ministry of Industry. There is a department called Department of Industrial Policy and Promotion at that time, now it is known as DPIIT. Yeah, and so at that time I used to work on small scale industries and so far as sustainability issues are concerned my MPhil research was on, because I was based in Shillong, Meghalaya. At that time my thesis itself was ecological implications of production and consumption pattern in Meghalaya. And that time, you know, I had done some estimation based on the current production and consumption, and we showed that within some 34 years the deforestation would take place in Meghalaya. And so I had presented this report to the government. And now I went go to state of Meghalaya,  Shillong. I can really see that the amount of deforestation will just taken place. Anyway, this was just by way of passing on some information. So far as a small scale, you know, nowadays we say small and medium enterprise rather we actually say Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises MSMEs, but small scale industry has been in the focus of the government action right from the beginning. In 1947, we gained freedom and in 1948 itself, there was this industrial policy resolution which had come up and in that industrial policy resolution some factors were defined where it was thought that we would be developing our industry to suit our national needs for that time. In 1951 and ACT was passed which was known as Industrial Development Act probably. And then in 1956, this act was Industrial Development and Regulation Act 1951. But actually the industrial policy, which should be seen very carefully is 1956 policy, which is 1956, industrial policy resolution. What it did was, you know, it categorized industries into three groups. First group was those industries, which would be completely owned and operationalized by the government. And the second group where both industry industries would be operationalized and owned jointly by public sector, and private sector. So, and then the third group was to be completely by the private sector. And in that private sector, the government would provide both the central and state government would provide all necessary support, including financial support and guidance. So in under the third category, which was to be absolutely of private sector, government created a certain institutions, they started working on it, and actually small scale industries fell under that category. So initially we thought that village and Khaadi Industries would be promoted through this and it would create a lot of employment for people and it would, because every state has some kind of, you know, specialization or handcraft and all this stuff. So every state would work and that would actually help, you know, having a balanced growth, you know, balanced development of industries and which will also create initial employment. And if that this happens, it'll also create initial, you know resource distribution equally. So that was the thought initially. And but there was a definition which had to be, come up with, and the definition was that industries we should have, you know investment of rupees, 5 lakh and below. Then either with using power and without using power. If they use power, they should have 50 people in their employment you know, list. Otherwise a 100 people would be employed without power. And that was a definition of small scale industry at that time. After that, you know, several times this investment limit has been, you know, changed. Actually upgraded 5 lakh, 10 lakh, 25 lakhs like that up to 2001. This continued to be increased but when in 2006 it was realized, actually some review was done at that time and it was realized that small scale industries have lot of scope, but they're not being able to realize the full potential. So, you know, a new act was brought in and that new act is Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises Development Act, which came into existence in 2006. And with that, the small scale industries got converted into micro, small and medium enterprises. And in that you know, all these three categories of industries were differentiated. After 2006 there were no, not much changes brought in, but it was all actions were being done in 2020. Investment limits were increased, you know, and in investment limits are increased. For micro industries, it was brought to rupees 1 crore, which are micro industries. And for small industries up to 10 crore. So between more than 1 crore up to 10 crore industries could be categorized at small industries and industries, which would be, you know, investing more than 10 crore. But below 50 crore could be considered as medium enterprises. So, and apart from this investment limit there was another alternative criteria which was brought in. So that alternative criteria is about their annual turnover. So certain industries were not being able to, you know, define themselves under the capital assets, investments. They would do the annual turnover, 2006, the investment limits was 25 lakh and small for 25 lakh to 5 crore and medium for 5 crore to 10 crore. So another important thing was, you know, the services sector was also brought under MSME. Earlier it was only manufacturing so thousand. What, could you, what could you attribute to this shift in the definitions, in, in the timeline? Is it, reflecting the external environment changes or the global benchmarking? Why did it change 2016? I would not say global benchmarking because globally, if you see, there is no uniform definition of small and medium enterprises anywhere. International institutions also define separately, EU defines separately, World Bank has some definition, and most of them would say a number of workers, you know rather than investment limits and all. Some people, of course some countries also attach investment limits and all that. This definition change services sector of course, you can imagine why we have brought, because services has really increased in India's economy. Services sector has expanded because at that time even now, you know, services sector contributes more than 50% for the last, maybe two decades, more than 20 years. Because after this IT revolution, which had come in India, you know, services sector has been contributing a lot. So services obviously had to be included, but services sector limits are different. And as you said, why these investment limits were changed, I would think these were changed because of the given the changes in the inflation and also given the change in the industrial environment and requirements. You know, because more industries you brought under these definition because there are certain schemes, certain incentives, certain tax incentives, some you know, financial loans, which were all extended to these sector. So because of some little bit of change here and there. It was not done, but of late. This latest definition, which has been changed in 2025, as you can say. The last one, which was done in April, 2025 and now actually small industry is an industry which has investment limit of 25 crore in their fixed assets means plant and machinery. And if they're looking at annual turnover, the small industries up to 100 crore of annual turnover would be considered as a small industry. Medium industries would be 125 crore for the investment limit and 500 crore for annual turnover. So this actually to broaden, you know, the industrial to upscale, you know, to help them scaling up, you know, their operation. And while at the same time getting the benefit of the incentives and all these things which government really want to push this sector. So this is so far as the definition parties concerned. If there is any clarification required, we can go over or so I had a question. Yeah, please. If, if our young learners are looking at this and they have kind of, they're currently in term four, so they have done a lot of courses in their earlier terms to understand how to build entrepreneurship models because this is a course on entrepreneurship. So when they look at these, the evolution of, you know, the SMEs in the Indian context, how should they be really thinking about sustainability in this context because it is very imperative today to embed sustainability in every thought process. And, and so, what sense should they be making of this and how will the their reading or their understanding of this help them to make sense? Because there is a lot of information out there. If they go to government official websites or if they try to understand it in a regional context, how should they be navigating the information overload? Right, that's a very important question, you know, because you know, even if a expert looks at the literature available or things which are happening people would be confused completely. But what I suggest is that, so that now we have gone to the, my own learning. You know, first I would clear my concept, what I am looking for. So I would suggest every student to actually look at the concepts very clearly. So when you said small scale industries and SMEs, so differences, so we explained a little bit. So when we talk about sustainability, again, there is no uniform definition. You know, sustainability also does not have an uniform definition because if you see some people may get confused with, you know, sustainable development goals. So if you go to sustainable development goals, they have 17 goals out of which 13 or some one goal is saying that we should actually address climate change. Right, so in the industrial context, sustainability is definitely related to climate change. This is what I would understand, but it would also refer to that the sustain, they should be sustainable in the long run. Yes, so sustainable in the long run means if you have to sustain one thing that every industry or every entrepreneur, when they start doing any business, they have to fulfill certain norms, regulations follow certain policy directions. So it is important to understand that perspective as well. Now, when we say that how SMEs or new enterprises can follow sustainability, I would actually say that they need to understand these three concepts again, which is used internationally as well as everywhere. So these three concepts are mitigation, adaptation, resilience. This book is really I found it useful. I would request students to particularly see one box that you have put in, understand this concept of sustainability and how it has evolved and this box, I think box page seven or something, page 7 to 12. They can, if they have the book, they can refer immediately. page 7 to 12, please have a look at that table, which wonderfully explains how this concept of climate change and sustainability debate started. And I think you have covered very long period right from when Steam engine was started and from that onwards since covered till 2021 COP. And from that table only I looked at, you know, it gave very interesting information at an interval of how many years population, you know, in increased 1 billion population because population has a very direct link to entire sustainability things. So to address your question if you are interested, we can just have a quick look at the population slide also. But this directly does not of course relate to this, but it is better to understand what's happening. You know, I think you also might not have noted at an interval of how many years, you know, population is increasing. But I really like the way you have tabulated the entire development of this debate. And I think that is very important because when we talk about economics you know, and we talk about scale it's important to think about the population not as a burden also, but as a human capital index. That is an opportunity for our young learners to also think about job creation in their entrepreneurial journey. And which is what you also mentioned initially with SMEs, how many jobs they could be creating in their, small setup that they have. So we have this slide on the pattern of the population. Yeah, yeah, yeah. This slide I have derived from your table. That box. So that's why I would like to also bring it to the notice of the students as well as your retention. You know, the first 1 billion population took 1800 years. The, so world had 80, took 1800 years to add 1 billion population, and the next billion gets added in 130 years. You see that the third billion took only 30 years, and 4 billion in 1975 took only 15 years, and 87 we reached. We raised 5 billion. That means it took 12 years, you know? And after that, you know, consistently we took around 12 years to add 1 billion population. And in 2022 we crossed 8. And now according to the estimated population, we are 8.23. And if we go by this rate, maybe it'll be 20, 34 or 35 that we cross 9 billion. So, why this table is important is because, you know, arts resources like this also indicate how much pressure we are creating on the earth. Because of this fast increase in population, of course recently, if you see this latest trend 2011 onwards, I think there is some initiative which has been taken policy initiative to reduce, you know, the population. And that might have slowed down the population growth a little bit, but it is still growing. So population is the most important. You have to take care of the people. So this is one indicator. We just wanted to bring this to the notice because that is at the back of our mind how much we are contributing because it means everything, you know. You, you have more population. You need more resources, you have more human capital. You also need more stringent planning, how those resources should be used. So population is definitely a very important indicator according to me. And so this is it. So going back to the basic, you know, that you were asking the question. So I'll again bring these definitions, of mitigation, adaptation, and resilience. You know, these are the three main concepts. Every climate discussion will you know, address these three concepts. So mitigation is you'll reduce the carbon emissions while at the same time, you know, if you can already absorb some emitted carbon. So that is mitigation. You are reducing carbon on the one side by you know, using some sophisticated technology or different means, or reducing consumption, whatever. But you are also trying to develop something which will absorb the already emitted carbon from the atmosphere. So mitigation is kind of, you know, netting out. And from this netting out came the concept of net zero. And in that net zero countries, at the international level, countries have committed for Net zero. So some countries have committed 20, 30, 20, 50, 20, whatever they suited, it was according to the national priorities. And India has committed by 2070, we'll have a net zero status because we are still developing. We have huge population here. We have we are still developing and a lot of industries, everything has to be done. So 2070 was estimated as a achievable thing. But of course the government is always trying its best to achieve it even earlier than 2017. So most of the commitments you will see that nationally determined commitments that we have. Those we have said by 2030 we'll achieve X, Y, Z. And that also there is, I think, another slide which I'll show. But this concept of mitigation means you are netting out whatever carbon emission is you were creating and while at the same time you were doing something so that emitted carbon can be absorbed back in the atmosphere. So that is mitigation. Second concept is about adaptation, which we have already said. This is even if you understand literal meaning of the term adaptation is adapting to, you know changes to making life changes. You know, so that actual or expected future climate requirements can be met. So you're actually adapting yourself to the climate requirements. So that is the concept of adaptation. So you are trying to make ecological, social, economic processes, practices, structures everywhere. You were trying to make changes to adapt to the climate change, so. This is the second concept, and the third concept is about resilience. This is the ultimate when we talk about resilience, this actually means sustainability. So if you are resilient, you know, you are able to adapt, absorb, and access all those critical services and products without, you know having implication for the, climate change. So that is resilience. Now, since your students are very young, if you wish to, you know, talk about even what climate change means, I don't know whether they are exposed to this. So actually, you know, this graph, which I was mentioning on the this graph. I'll just move a little bit. What it is showing is that, you know this temperature carbon emission remained up to a level, you know, it's below 300 ppm was the, they call it PPM and after 1950, you know, it suddenly jumped. So earlier. Also, there were some ups and downs. Those ups and downs were like natural due to natural processes. There are some changes happening, those are natural processes. But after 1950s, it just suddenly jumped. And this jump is said that this jump is because of manmade, you know, intervention. And this is what we shall have to reduce. So now, according to this graph is taken from NASA website finaly. Okay, so NASA has a website and of course everyone is working. All re reputed institutions are working on climate change and sustainability. So there is al also this World Resource Institute, so people can go there and see how climate change are being measured and all those are very interesting things. So you have these concepts called mitigation, adaptation, and resilience. So ultimately people have to aim at resilience. So how do you aim at resilience? So when somebody has to start, suppose you are starting a new business, you are actually even in an advantageous position because you already know that there is sustainability issue, which you have to take care of. But if there is an industry already existing, they have to do lot of adjustment and adaptation. That's right. So anyone joining entering the entrepreneurship journey as a fresher has an advantage here because even I think you know, when you go and finance options, the banks or the financial institutions will always ask you what is your plan for coping with climate resilience? And, and that is where they have an opportunity to also present their ideas in such a way. So they. Are showing that they're aware that climate extremities can impact business. And they are set up and they have taken that into account while working on this proposal that they plan to get financed. Absolutely. And actually, you know, what I would say is that internationally, globally, also in fact Indian small scale industry is around 30% of GDP is contributed by the SMEs in India. But globally, you know, their contribution is much higher. Internationally, it is said that around 90% even World Economic Forum or even reports or all those reports will show that around 90% of their GDPs are actually contributed by the small and medium enterprises. So in case of India, I don't know whether this measurement might change because of the latest definition change. Earlier what because media enterprises would now cover a lot of more industries earlier they were just not covered. They were part of large industries. So by existing definition before April, 2025, around 30% of GDP was contributed by small scale industries. So when globally it is 90%, India's small scale industries are giving only 30%. So obviously there is a scope to export. And scope to create better productivity and efficiency. And for that, you know I think government is doing a lot to promote. And one thing if anybody is trying to start a new business should keep in mind that there is this scheme called zero defect, zero effect. You know, this is what I think Prime Minister had spoken about, and then it has become a scheme. Okay, zero effect. Zero effect means when you produce something, it should be absolutely free from any defect. That should be the and you should have zero effect means you should have no effect on environment. On environment that is directly linked to sustainability. So, so when you have new industries and new industries coming up, or new entrepreneur coming up to work for them, there is a lot of guidance and handhold, right? Right. But for the existing ones, there's a problem. There are issues and they have to look for resources. Which one they should do. And there of course many issues are there to sort out. So, yeah. Please. So how can then SMEs orient themselves, the existing ones towards policy direction because they keep changing very frequently, depending on the country determined indicators and the COP summits. What, what comes out of that? So how could you know, SMEs orient themselves towards policy direction, especially when it comes to renewables or if there is a directive that, like for instance, India is leading the a solar alliance. And, and that is a push towards that or green hydrogen. So how do you if you can, through some case studies, tell us how industries should look at, or SME should orient themselves towards such policy directions. It'll give our learners a sense or a starting point from where they could probably look. So solar reliance is something very interesting. You mentioned it was initiated by India. I think when this Paris climate deals were done. India had launched you know, this in international solar reliance. It was a new idea. And now 120 countries, or maybe more, at least 120 countries are member of solar reliance. And because of solar reliance, you know, our energy generation from solar has increased so much. Now, this renew out of renewable energy around 48% is from, only from solar, even more than hydropower. You know? So that's quite an achievement. That's a very good achievement. You know? And so when you talk about a new industry, first of all, let us understand, you know, what is the role of this small industries or small and medium enterprises, you know, medium enterprises? I would think that they're still growing big. But small enterprises are basically, once I visited I think in Karnataka only one factory, you know, where they were manufacturing only motherboard of computers. In another place we saw somebody manufacturing only these SIMs, you know, phone, SIM cards. You know, so when we have the finished product with us, we, we don't realize that how much products have gone into it. So entire global value chain has been developed because of that. There are very few big industries who are actually, you know obtaining these smaller products from different countries and industries. Those are the importance of small scale industries. You know, they, because those small industries are providing smaller parts and components, so small industries, basic role is parts and components and well in the value chain. So why we called about zero defect, you know, because lot of Indian industries could not satisfy their international standard. That's why they have not been part of the value chain, because of which we lost a lot of trade. We lost export opportunities, you know, and you'll see smaller countries like Vietnam, Bangladesh, and many other, all East Asian countries, they got integrated with the bigger value chain through their small scale industries. And so why small scale industries, again, need to look at sustainability is because these bigger industries have already committed to sustainability and they have to fulfill those requirements. Even in India, you know, we have this business business responsibility, BRSR, right? Business responsibility and sustainability reporting. Reporting internationally. Are still doing ESG, environmental, social and governance reporting, correct. In that they have to give the declaration how much they are doing to maintain sustainability for their companies under these three heads, great. In India, we are also taking care of you know, the responsibility of the business. Okay, so, so this you'll find the students can look at some of the actually file BRSR report. At least for Infosys, I think I have given one slide where you will find useful references and in that slide one can see the link where Infos has given its BRSR report and how it has done. So this will be useful for somebody to see. If you see, if the students can see how actually the report is, you know, done, how the report has to be they have to fulfill the requirements of the reporting. Yes, that will help them in understanding the subject. But what is important is that it is not the reporting only, which is important, you know, it is actually, you know, adopting or adapting those practices in your industry. there is another case, example of which I think I have given, again, a link in the reference of the website where one real industry, how it is moving towards sustainability, you know, that also will help the students in doing that. Looking into the details, I just thought I would not go into all those details because that they can look for their own, homework or for their own paper or something. It is better that they look into those, you know. So this is another respect. So apart from that, you know, if I think your book also has. One very important reference about the news reports, okay. Right. So small industries and their issues come up on and off in the news reports also. And with sustainability, those reports actually, you'll see that a lot of mining activities have been stopped by some activist group. And all those kind of reports come. So when people read those reports. Everybody, all students here also, when they read the news reports, my suggestion would be to understand the background in totality. You know, news report because reporter has seen certain thing and he has reported, and that gives us a clue, you know, what is happening. And then if we're interested in the subject, we would go into the details. And see exactly and go to the original source. I would say that news reports should not be taken by by their face value. One should always have a look. You know, this is what I always do, and I always tell everyone that whatever you report, you know, even nowadays I have seen youngsters should come up saying that this I've seen this from Wikipedia. So according to me, you know, Wikipedia as not an authentic source. You know, Wikipedia is somebody's observation or somebody's own knowledge, individual knowledge. Okay, so I know about professor Deepti. So I write a profile for the Deepti, professor Deepti, and that profile is how much I know about it. Okay, so it is not that. It is a complete profile. It is a genuine profile. I may be making a lot of mistakes in writing while your profile. So similarly, you know, even industries reports are not genuine all the time. So, and sometimes, you know, there, there are here and there some changes would be made, right. So in the news report one has to see and one has to understand that no, but what is recommended is actually internationally also, and nationally also, when somebody starts, they should start looking at those ESG framework or BRSR framework in India, what are those requirements? And this is not for compliance only as I said, if you want to run your business, you have to know those and you have to fulfill those requirements. If you want to go for doing business with a bigger company because that bigger company will ask you. Because I think one company from Germany, I was reading, you know, one Italian company even a company Ikea. All those companies, you know, they're asking for you to certify when they're procuring some inputs from you or some components from you, or some parts from you. You have to certify that you have, you know, fulfill the sustainability requirements. So until, and unless you give the declaration, they would not buy your products. So then you are losing out on that business as well. And also if you, the government of India is also giving certain, say, tax benefit. So if you are not having sustainable practices, you would not get that tax benefit. So those are the real practical things which people have to understand and even for financing. There is I think last time when we had discussed we had gone into all these details, and I think you have another session which might be discussing all this. So we are not going there. So this is regarding, you know, how a bank can ask you, bank can ask these small investors to give sustainability declaration, right. Right. So what happened now is that BRSR says that top 1000 BSE listed companies have to file this report. So it is most likely that small industries are not covered here, most probably. Some small industries, if they have listed in the BSE they might be covered, but they are not likely to appear in the top thousand. But that does not mean that small industries do not have to fulfill sustainability. As I said, if they do not fulfill this, they'll not be able to integrate in the system. So that is a very a critical thing for everyone. So where from to start I, I'll always suggest start looking at ESG framework or BRSR framework for India and see what exactly you have to fulfill and that will really help. I think I think these are great starting points for our students because there's so much of information you know, that is coming out of this discussion. And the magnitude of it as well, because all our students are spread across the country. They're coming from different regions and every region has a, a specialty like you just mentioned about. Your initial days in Meghalaya, in Shillong and, and how that fed into your you know, your work as well. So I think you know, it is also important in a country like India to kind of look into the policy directions in the specific local region and understand the ecosystem and then also try to align your entrepreneurship goals towards that. Absolutely and I actually had noted some more points from the book because I think they have to read this book and they should actually first of all, I would say that this code you have done very aptly which is the code of our Gandhiji and in the first chapter itself. You said that art provides enough to satisfy every man's need, whatnot the greed, this is the, you know, this is, see, he, at that time only he realized that how important sustainability is and Gandhi of course, won appearance, reveals a lot, you know, what kind of clothing he used to wear and all this. So that is that really, one should look forward to and, you know, practice in your own life. There is also a program by the government called LiFE. Lifestyle for Sustainable sustainable lifestyle, something like that. So in the life we are saying, of course, Indians do a lot of thing for living. Actually we are the most sustainable, I would think, you know, given my love for my country and after seeing so many different countries in the world, I think we are really a country where resources are used very, very conservatively compared to what internationally or developed countries have been. Great, see we, they have a practice of use and throw. And some of that practice started coming in our country. But fortunately we realized the importance of sustainability. And I think we are again, giving up. That practice, you know, use intro practice is not a good one. There is also this concept of circular economy, which has been brought in internationally as well as in India. So this circular economy. The circular economy, the idea is that you recycle the things. You reuse a thing and you use it. That is for industry as well as for your individual living. So life and circular economy are the two concepts which I think are very important for everyone to internalize for that our personal living, our business, our every practice, we should internalize these two things. To make maximum out of available resources. Right. And is there any suggestion that you could think about or insights that you derived while reading the book where young students can think about co-producing news along with the local entrepreneurship ecosystem to help local media, you know, the regional or the vernacular media, as we call them cover specific issues specific to that region because of the narrative almost always goes by the global trends or what other newspapers nationally are covering. They just get translated into the local language. But we have various media outlets, like even a community radio in village, right. Or a person who is a YouTuber or someone who is having access to a medium that he or she chooses to depict in that specific region. How could our young learners try to collaborate with them in order to make sure that they understand what are the issues that are coming up there in that ecosystem? Absolutely and I think your study relates to Western Ghats and you actually went and interviewing people and you in one table you have given their responses. And you know, immediately when I looked at the response I thought, oh my God, this is self can be analyzed. You know, there are all those concepts which are coming there. I think I'll, this book, the book is in front of me. If you can see page 77, if students have their books, they can open page 77, table 6.1. You know, there you have actually given some responses, overview of the community responses to climate and socioeconomic change. Yes, so, you know, because community is not seeing it as a sustainability issue, but you as a researcher could relate what they're seeing and how it should be put in that particular, you know, relativity.
